---
title: "Project Proposal"
subtitle: "INF2167 - R for Data Science"
author:
  - "Jocelyn Cao"
  - "Harry Parmar"
format: pdf
execute: 
  warning: false
---

# Introduction

The City of Toronto has a target to reach 40% tree canopy cover by 2050, and currently, the city has approximately 28-31% canopy cover and must bridge the roughly 10% gap (City of Toronto, 2025). This project will explore the properties of street tree data sourced from the City of Toronto and evaluate how well-prepared the city is to meet its goal considering risk factors, such as monoculture vulnerability and high mortality rates among small trees.

# Research Questions

This project will aim to answer the following question: does Toronto have enough young trees in its street tree population to realistically expand the canopy by 2050?

While Toronto is planting plenty of small trees, most of them are dying young due to harsh roadside conditions before they can grow large enough to contribute to the city's canopy goal. Due to this bottleneck, the current street tree population is likely not on track to hit the 40% canopy target by 2050.

Note that this analysis only looks at street tree data. Because it leaves out trees on private property and in city parks, the city's overall canopy outlook may be healthier than this specific dataset depicts.

# Relevant Literature

The significance of urban forest diversity and structural stability is well-documented in recent literature, providing a framework to evaluate the City of Toronto’s progress toward its 2050 canopy goals. This study draws on three primary areas of research: taxonomic diversity, diameter at breast height (DBH) distributions, and urban tree mortality.

## Taxonomic Diversity (The 10-20-30 Rule)

Urban forests are often less diverse than natural forests, making them more vulnerable to catastrophic losses from pests and diseases (Freiburger et al., 2025). To combat this, practitioners frequently use the 10-20-30 rule, which suggests that a city’s tree population should consist of no more than 10% of any single species, 20% of any genus, or 30% of any family.

## DBH Distributions

The size distribution of a tree population, typically measured by DBH, serves as a vital proxy for age and structural diversity. Morgenroth et al. (2020) categorize urban forest structures into three types: Type I (youthful), Type II (maturing), and Type III (mature). See Figure 1.

![Figure 1: Type I, Type II, and Type III structural distributions (Morgenroth et al., 2020)](images\figure1.jpg){width=50%}

A youthful distribution, characterized by a high proportion of small, young trees, is often considered ideal for population stability as it ensures a "reverse J" curve where young trees are recruited to replace older ones. Understanding where Toronto’s street trees fall on this spectrum is essential for predicting whether the current population can realistically mature into a 40% canopy cover.

## Mortality Risks

The success of canopy expansion efforts is heavily dependent on tree survival, particularly during the first five years after planting (Hilbert et al., 2019). High mortality among small trees can undermine planting initiatives, meaning that a youthful population does not guarantee a future mature canopy unless stewardship and maintenance are prioritized to overcome these early losses.

# Dataset & Methods

# Exploratory Data Analysis

# References

City of Toronto. (2026, June 4). Street tree data [Data set]. Toronto Open Data. https://open.toronto.ca/dataset/street-tree-data/

City of Toronto. (2025, February 12). Strengthening Toronto's tree canopy: Report on climate resilience and tree equity (Report for Action). Environment, Climate and Forestry

Freiburger, M. R., Murphy-Dunning, C., Doroski, D. A., Ashton, P. M., & Peters, J. D. J. (2025). The 10/20/30 planting rule aligns with traditional plant diversity metrics across spatial scales. Urban Forestry and Urban Greening, 114. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ufug.2025.129112

Hilbert, D. R., Roman, L. A., Koeser, A. K., Vogt, J., & van Doorn, N. S. (2019). Urban tree mortality: A literature review. Arboriculture and Urban Forestry, 45(5), 167–200. https://doi.org/10.48044/jauf.2019.015

Morgenroth, J., Nowak, D. J., & Koeser, A. K. (2020). DBH Distributions in America’s Urban Forests—An Overview of Structural Diversity. Forests, 11(2), 135. https://doi.org/10.3390/f11020135

R Core Team. (2024). R: A language and environment for statistical computing. R Foundation for Statistical Computing. https://www.R-project.org/
